skill.name: quetrex-development-workflow
skill.description: Bootstraps Claude Code and Codex sessions with Quetrex project context, issue workflows, quality gates, and automation guidance.
skill.summary: Start Quetrex development sessions with project context, GitHub workflow commands, and quality standards.
skill.author: Barnhardt-Enterprises-Inc

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command-like strings, but review shows they are GitHub CLI and git workflow examples for a Quetrex development process. The Windows SAM and weak cryptography alerts are false positives from ordinary project text and filenames. The skill is publishable with a warning because it instructs users to run external commands that can modify GitHub state.

medium_findings.title: External GitHub Workflow Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill provides commands such as GitHub issue creation, workflow execution, PR inspection, PR merge, and log download. These commands are legitimate for the stated workflow, but they can alter repository state or retrieve CI artifacts when run with an authenticated GitHub CLI session.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicitly present and include state-changing GitHub CLI operations. The surrounding context is workflow documentation, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: High-Severity Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and Windows SAM static alerts do not correspond to cryptographic operations or Windows credential access. The reviewed lines contain a skill description, issue example text, TDD workflow text, and a project memory filename.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines were inspected directly and contain no credential store access or cryptographic API usage. The matches appear to come from incidental words in documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: State-Changing CLI Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents commands that can create issues, trigger GitHub Actions workflows, edit labels, and merge pull requests. Users should confirm repository, branch, and permissions before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clear and executable in a normal shell with GitHub CLI installed. They are not hidden or obfuscated, and their purpose is documented.

content.value_statement: Quetrex sessions need current project context before useful development work can begin. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a concise workflow map for issues, automation, quality gates, and project status.
actual_capabilities: Explains the Quetrex product purpose, core capabilities, and technology stack.
Shows how AI-agent issues trigger GitHub Actions and Claude Code automation.
Provides examples for creating well-scoped issues with acceptance criteria.
Summarizes quality gates, testing expectations, and development patterns.
Points users to project status, memory files, architecture docs, and checklists.
limitations: It is tailored to the Quetrex repository structure and naming conventions.
It depends on GitHub CLI access for live issue, PR, and workflow commands.
It does not verify whether referenced project files are current.
It guides workflow decisions but does not enforce repository permissions.

use_cases.title: Start a Quetrex Coding Session
use_cases.description: Load project context, identify pending work, and choose whether to create an agent issue or work directly.

use_cases.title: Prepare AI-Agent Issues
use_cases.description: Write focused feature requests with acceptance criteria, file references, test expectations, and priority levels.

use_cases.title: Review Agent Automation Output
use_cases.description: Inspect AI-generated pull requests, check workflow runs, review logs, and apply the documented quality gates.

prompt_templates.title: Begin a Session
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Quetrex development workflow to summarize the project, list the current workflow steps, and identify the first safe action.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Agent Issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me turn this feature idea into a Quetrex AI-agent issue with clear acceptance criteria, relevant files, tests, and priority.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Pull Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Quetrex workflow to review an AI-agent pull request. Focus on quality gates, tests, architecture fit, and merge readiness.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a Failed Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through debugging a failed Quetrex AI-agent workflow run. Identify the likely failure source and the next verification steps.

output_examples.input: Start a Quetrex session and tell me what to check first.
output: Review open ai-feature issues and recent merged pull requests.
Check the status file for the current roadmap phase.
Choose direct development only when the feature is complex or needs close control.

output_examples.input: Convert a dashboard cost display idea into an AI-agent issue.
output_examples.output: A structured issue with summary, description, acceptance criteria, technical context, testing requirements, and priority.

output_examples.input: Help me review an AI-agent pull request.
output: Inspect the pull request summary and changed files.
Check tests, linting, build results, and project conventions before merge.

best_practices: Confirm the target repository and branch before running GitHub CLI commands.
Use focused issues with acceptance criteria, file references, and test expectations.
Review AI-agent pull requests with the same standards as human-authored changes.
anti_patterns: Do not combine unrelated features into one AI-agent issue.
Do not merge generated pull requests before reviewing tests and changed files.
Do not rely on stale status documents without checking live issues and pull requests.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps AI coding tools understand the Quetrex development workflow and start productive sessions faster.

faq.question: Does it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents commands and workflows that a user or agent may choose to run.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: It supports Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: Why does it mention GitHub CLI commands?
faq.answer: Quetrex uses GitHub issues, pull requests, and Actions workflows to coordinate AI-agent development.

faq.question: Can it be used outside Quetrex?
faq.answer: Only as a reference. The file paths, labels, and workflow names are specific to Quetrex.

faq.question: What should users check before using it?
faq.answer: Users should confirm repository access, GitHub CLI authentication, current status files, and active pull requests.
